identity.program_name: Master of Information Management and Systems
identity.credential_name: Master of Information Management and Systems (MIMS)

positioning.positioning_summary: UC Berkeley’s MIMS is positioned as a two-year, full-time professional master’s for aspiring technology leaders and information professionals, with a strongly interdisciplinary curriculum spanning computer science, data systems/AI, human-centered design/UX, policy, ethics, law, and management. The program also signals a practical, career-oriented focus through hands-on project work, internships, and outcomes in UX, product, data, and tech leadership, while explicitly framing its work around ethical technology and social impact.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIMS is a two-year, full-time, 48-semester-unit professional degree. The first year is mostly a required core, followed by electives and additional breadth requirements in the second year, with a required final project in the last semester. The program is flexible overall and supports specialization through electives and thematic focus areas, but it does not present formal tracks or concentrations.

electives.summary: Electives are broad and flexible, with students drawing from the I School catalog and other UC Berkeley departments to build specialization in areas such as UX, data science, policy, entrepreneurship, and privacy/security. The program requires at least 40 units from the Info course catalog, may include up to 6 units of individual or group study, and allows up to 8 units from other departments to count toward the degree.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course introducing information organization and retrieval. Covers conceptual modeling, semantic representation, vocabulary and metadata design, classification, standardization, and information retrieval practices and applications.
learning_outcomes: Understand foundations of information organization and retrieval
Apply conceptual modeling and semantic representation
Design vocabularies, metadata, and classification schemes
Use information retrieval practices and related technologies
Analyze textual and non-textual information computationally

core_courses.course_summary: Required 3-unit core course introducing social issues around information and information technology, including technology’s impact on society and social groups.
learning_outcomes: Explain how information technologies affect social groups and social interaction.
Analyze how society shapes the technologies that are produced.
Discuss theories of technology in society.
Identify workplace, automation, and networked sociability issues.
Use social scientific methods to examine information technology problems.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course examining legal and ethical issues in information and communication technologies, including privacy, freedom of expression, consumer protection, cybersecurity, and copyright.
learning_outcomes: Analyze legal and ethical issues in information technologies.
Explain how policy frameworks shape technology design and use.
Evaluate privacy, copyright, and consumer protection concerns.
Reflect on the ethical impact of information professionals' work.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course in programming and computation. The 2-unit course introduces Python programming for computer science, data science, and information management, with emphasis on problem decomposition, program efficiency, and good programming style.
learning_outcomes: Write interactive Python programs
Analyze data and process text
Draw graphics and manipulate images
Simulate physical systems
Apply problem decomposition and efficient coding practices

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to Data Structures and Analytics
core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing data structures and data analysis in Python; 2 units; forms the second half of the INFO 206A/206B sequence.
learning_outcomes: Represent structured data sets
Manipulate and analyze data in Python
Apply best practices for writing code
Build on INFO 206A prerequisites

core_courses.course_title: Directed Group Work on Final Project
core_courses.course_summary: Required culminating final project completed in the fourth semester. The project integrates skills and concepts from the MIMS program and may take the form of a research paper or professionally oriented applied work.
learning_outcomes: Integrate skills and concepts learned in the MIMS program.
Formulate and carry out a sustained, coherent project.
Produce a tangible work product.
Manage a significant project from start to finish.

positioning.positioning_summary: The Berkeley MDes is positioned as a three-semester, interdisciplinary professional graduate program in design for emerging technologies that connects technical rigor, design theory, and social practice, with strong emphasis on career preparation, real-world application, and socially responsible design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Berkeley MDes is a studio-based, three-semester professional master’s program completed over about 16 months and requiring a minimum of 39 units. The curriculum combines eight named required core courses, a summer Design@Large professional development experience, and four electives drawn from technical, entrepreneurship, social practice, and open UC Berkeley courses. The materials show a clear year-by-year sample sequence, but the program also allows some flexibility in elective timing and selection.

electives.summary: The MDes requires four electives: one technical elective, one entrepreneurship or social practice elective, and two open electives. The approved lists are broad and cross-disciplinary, spanning computing, engineering, planning, new media, and design-related topics. The open electives may be any 3- or 4-unit upper-division or graduate UC Berkeley course, subject to availability. The structure supports individualized emphasis, but the program does not advertise formal tracks or concentrations.

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational MDes core course that interweaves practical design methods with readings and lectures on the history of design and technology.

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational course in design and technology frameworks. Students use human-centered design, research, concept generation, prototyping, critique, and a semester-long hardware-plus-digital project to create a product-service system.

core_courses.course_summary: Colloquium-style course exploring a yearly design topic through invited lectures, readings, discussion, and written reflections.

identity.credential_name: Master of Fine Arts in Media Design Practices

positioning.positioning_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is positioned as an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated design program centered on emerging technologies, critical frameworks, and experimental studio practice. It emphasizes research, prototyping, and fieldwork, with explicit social-impact and global-context framing, and prepares students for professional roles at the intersection of technology, culture, and design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated graduate program organized around a sequenced studio curriculum leading to thesis work. The evidence indicates two- and three-year pathways, with the three-year option adding preparatory undergraduate-level terms before the standard MGx track. The program emphasizes critical thinking, creative technology, prototyping, research, fieldwork, and thesis studio work; no distinct concentration tracks are clearly described in the fetched material.

electives.summary: The program overview does not describe a formal elective structure or named elective categories for Media Design Practices. The evidence only indicates a sequenced studio curriculum with a thesis year and additional preparatory terms in the three-year option, but it does not clearly state how electives are selected or whether they support specialization.

core_courses.course_summary: First-term development-year course in the three-year MDP track focused on critical framing and worldview analysis; 3 units.
learning_outcomes: Analyze critical worldviews shaping media design practice.
Frame design problems through critical and cultural perspectives.
Develop a reflective stance toward technology and society.
Connect design decisions to broader social and ethical contexts.

core_courses.course_summary: Fall-term thesis-writing and dissemination course in the Media Design Practices MFA. Students develop the written component of the thesis, situate their work in discourse, and prepare materials for sharing design practice through venues such as social media, journals, and biennales.
learning_outcomes: Develop the written component of a thesis
Use writing as a generative design tool
Situate work within critical discourse
Translate practice for public dissemination
Prepare thesis-related promotional materials

core_courses.course_summary: A required creative technology studio term in the MDP sequence, focused on emerging technologies, prototyping, and experimental making.
learning_outcomes: Investigate emerging technologies in design practice.

Use research and fieldwork to inform design decisions.

core_courses.course_summary: Thesis-year studio in the three-year Media Design Practices track, where students develop thesis work after the preparatory terms.
learning_outcomes: Develop thesis work through studio practice
Apply research and fieldwork to design problems
Use emerging technologies in experimental making
Create new modes of storytelling and installation

identity.credential_name: Master of Science in Computational Design

positioning.positioning_summary: Carnegie Mellon’s Computational Design program is positioned as a highly interdisciplinary, research-focused graduate program at the nexus of design and computation. It emphasizes critical technical practice, novel research contributions, and work that spans architecture, engineering, art, humanities, and computing, while also foregrounding emerging technologies, digital methods, physical fabrication, and socially and environmentally aware design inquiry.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Computational Design program is a two-year, research-focused MS/PhD pathway centered on interdisciplinary study at the intersection of design and computation. The evidence describes a lean sequence of research seminars, selective courses, special topics, independent studies, and a thesis, with students building individualized plans of study under faculty guidance. The program emphasizes technical, conceptual, historical, and critical inquiry, and the MS culminates in a substantial thesis.

electives.summary: Electives are highly flexible but guided by faculty and the program’s research orientation. The MS curriculum uses research seminars, selective courses, special topics seminars, independent studies, and a broad pool of eligible extra-departmental courses to let students build a personalized path; the evidence does not describe fixed elective buckets or named elective lists for this program page.

identity.program_name: Master of Design in Design for Interactions

positioning.positioning_summary: The MDes in Design for Interactions is positioned as a rigorous, research-informed design program for students with prior design backgrounds and professional experience, centered on interaction design, systems thinking, design research, and thesis work. It emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration, professional practice, and social impact through services, social innovation, sustainability, and future-oriented design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master of Design in Design for Interactions is a two-year, four-semester program built around required studios, seminars, labs, thesis preparation, and electives. The curriculum is described semester by semester, with first-year study focused on interaction, services, and social innovation, and second-year study centered on thesis research, development, and public defense. The page also indicates that students may take electives in the School of Design or across Carnegie Mellon, including outside design, to complement their skills and knowledge.

electives.summary: Electives are flexible rather than locked to a single list: students take a design elective or a course outside design in the first year, and again in the second year, to complement their skills and knowledge. The page recommends electives in policy, business, service or social innovation, interaction or communication design, and professional writing, and also notes that students may take courses across Carnegie Mellon, including in other schools and departments.

core_courses.course_summary: First-year core study in the MDes program introduces interaction design practice, design research, systems thinking, and methods for designing services and social innovation concepts.
learning_outcomes: Understand interaction design practice in context
Apply design research methods to complex systems

core_courses.course_summary: Project-based course on service design fundamentals, including analyzing and improving existing services and developing new service experiences. Students work across digital interactions and physical experiences, hypothesize, experiment, build, test, pivot, and pitch a service with value to a particular audience.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamentals of service design

core_courses.course_summary: The final semester brings the thesis project to fruition through synthesis, documentation, public presentation, and defense.

positioning.positioning_summary: Columbia GSAPP’s M.S. in Computational Design Practices is positioned as an interdisciplinary, professionally oriented program that bridges architecture, data visualization, and urban planning, with a strong STEM-adjacent and research-inflected focus on computational design for the built environment. The program also emphasizes civic, activist, and social-impact applications, including equitable ways of imagining and creating space.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The M.S. in Computational Design Practices is organized around a prescribed sequence of colloquia, foundation coursework, and electives. The degree requirements page shows a term-by-term plan with an online pre-program summer, summer, fall, and spring components, and the program page describes three colloquia culminating in a final capstone project. Students must complete at least 45 credits, with required foundation courses and electives spanning GSAPP offerings.

electives.summary: Electives are built into the program and span GSAPP offerings, allowing students to choose courses across the school and related areas. The degree requirements page shows elective credits in both the pre-program summer and summer term, and the program overview says electives create bridges and collaborations across shared concerns. The curriculum is flexible within this structured framework, but the program does not describe distinct specialization tracks.

host_academic_units: Guarini School of Graduate and Advanced Studies

positioning.positioning_summary: The program is positioned as a computer science master’s concentration that bridges CS with digital arts and visual computing. It emphasizes a rigorous CS foundation, foundational digital arts coursework, and research/thesis work in areas such as computer graphics, HCI, digital fabrication, digital art and media, computer vision, VR, and AR. The framing is strongly interdisciplinary, STEM-adjacent, research-oriented, and connected to digital/fine-arts practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MS in Computer Science with a concentration in Digital Arts is described as a two-year program that combines technical computer science study, digital arts coursework, and research/thesis work. Evidence indicates a thesis-based graduate path with a required thesis defense, but the fetched material does not provide a full term-by-term plan or a complete named course list for the concentration.

electives.summary: The concentration is described as having three areas—technical courses, digital art courses, and research/thesis courses—so coursework is structured rather than fully open. The evidence does not provide a detailed elective menu or category rules for the concentration itself, but it does show that students combine computer science and digital arts study and complete thesis research as part of the program.

positioning.positioning_summary: TU Delft’s MSc Integrated Product Design is positioned as an advanced, research-informed design program that blends design, technology, and engineering to create user-centered, innovative, sustainable, and ethical products and product-service combinations. The program also explicitly highlights emerging technologies such as AI, smart textiles, bio-design, digital fabrication, and computational design, while emphasizing balance among user, business, and societal interests.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MSc Integrated Product Design is a two-year, 120 ECTS programme with a structured first-year core, a theme-based second-year component, a 30 ECTS free elective semester, and a 30 ECTS graduation project. The curriculum is organized around faculty core courses, programme core courses, design studios, three theme courses chosen from an offer, and flexible electives that can include faculty courses, other TU Delft or Dutch university courses, internships, or study abroad.

electives.summary: The programme includes a 30 ECTS free elective space in the third semester. Students can fill it with IDE faculty electives, courses from other TU Delft faculties or Dutch universities, an internship, or study abroad. The elective space is described as a way to broaden, deepen, or further specialize the student’s profile, and the programme materials also point to theme-based elective options and approved elective offerings.

positioning.positioning_summary: Harvard GSD’s Master in Design Studies is positioned as a research-based, interdisciplinary design program focused on urgent contemporary issues and social impact. The program emphasizes design research and scholarship, flexible domain-based study, and work that connects design to public service, academia, NGOs, and advanced practice; the Publics domain further highlights socio-spatial design, planning, advocacy, and the common good.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master in Design Studies is a four-semester, 72-unit program organized by domain (Ecologies, Mediums, Narratives, or Publics). Students complete a required first-semester proseminar in their chosen domain, a 12-unit set of approved distributional electives in the first year, a required 8-unit collaborative open project in the third semester, and a largely elective fourth semester. The curriculum is flexible within each domain but still has a clear semester-by-semester structure.

electives.summary: In the first year, students must take a minimum of 12 units of approved distributional electives from the list for their chosen domain, and those electives may be completed in the second year with Domain Head approval. The program is interdisciplinary and encourages cross-registration across the GSD, other Harvard schools, and MIT, while the fourth semester in the domain plans is largely or entirely general electives.

positioning.positioning_summary: IIT Institute of Design’s M.Des. is positioned as a STEM-designated, professionally oriented graduate design program for strategic designers who work across complex business, civic, and social challenges. The program emphasizes human-centered and systems design, collaborative real-world projects, and impact in private, public, and nonprofit sectors, while also welcoming applicants without formal design training.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The M.Des. is a two-year, 54-credit program with a structured core and substantial elective flexibility. Required study includes a small set of core courses, one 4-credit choice from a specified course set, and a large elective block drawn mainly from 500- and 600-level IDN/IDX courses. Students without prior design preparation may need Foundation courses, and some international students may take English + Design courses concurrently in the first two semesters. The curriculum is organized around user research, communication, systems thinking, strategy, and workshops, with adviser-guided course selection and optional outside-of-ID electives allowed in limited amounts.

electives.summary: Electives make up the majority of the degree and are selected with adviser guidance from any non-required 500-level or 600-level IDN or IDX course. Up to 12 credits may come from selected undergraduate IDN 481–497 courses if they are not also used as prerequisites, and up to 6 credits may be taken outside of ID with approval. The program also presents concentration-style modules in areas such as research, communication, interaction design, data and design, product/services design, collaboration, enterprise management, innovation, and civic systems, so electives clearly support specialization even though the degree does not appear to require a single formal track.

positioning.positioning_summary: Harvard’s Master in Design Engineering is positioned as a collaborative, interdisciplinary program that bridges design and engineering to tackle complex real-world and societal challenges. The program emphasizes systems-level problem solving, innovation, and leadership, with explicit ties to sustainability, urbanization, resource scarcity, and other large-scale public-interest issues.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program is a four-semester, highly structured curriculum organized by domain. Students choose one of four domains and complete a required first-semester proseminar, DES-3105 Futures By Design in the second semester, an 8-unit Open Project in the third semester, and general electives concentrated across the third and fourth semesters. The evidence also shows a minimum 12-unit distributional elective requirement drawn from approved domain lists, with cross-registration allowed but constrained.

electives.summary: Students must complete at least 12 units of distributional electives from approved domain-specific lists, with the option to finish some of them in the second year if the Domain Head approves. The program also allows general electives, and the evidence indicates that electives are used to build an individualized trajectory within the chosen domain rather than a fixed specialization track.

electives.summary: The program includes a cross-disciplinary minor component that takes up about a quarter of course time, and students can choose from thematic course directions such as applied and entrepreneurial work, critical and speculative work, or communicative and reflective work. The broader ZHdK major-minor model also allows students to choose one or two minors to deepen or expand their major skills across disciplines, but the sources do not provide a detailed elective rule set or named elective catalog courses.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MA Design in Knowledge Visualization is positioned as a design program focused on creating images for scientific knowledge processes, with explicit emphasis on visualizing complex information and scientific contexts. Its framing also highlights visualization and communication concepts, 3D technologies, and imaging processes, and the subject-area description stresses relevance for teaching, research, and the public. Overall, the strongest signals are research-oriented, STEM-adjacent, interdisciplinary, and socially relevant.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MA Design, Knowledge Visualization is a four-semester, research-oriented master’s program built around an individual thesis/project. positioning.positioning_summary: The RCA Service Design MA is positioned as a pioneering, research-informed, project-based design programme focused on service design as a tool for social and organisational change. It emphasizes real-world challenges, collaboration with commercial/public/social organisations, and preparation for leadership roles across diverse sectors, with a strong human-centred and systems-oriented orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Service Design MA is a 180-credit, one-year full-time programme structured across three terms with a clear sequence of core units and required project work. Students begin with foundation and practice units in Term 1, move into contextual and management-oriented work in Term 2, and complete a self-directed Independent Research Project in Term 3. The curriculum also includes cross-college/interdisciplinary electives that students choose alongside the core pathway.

electives.summary: Electives are structured but flexible: students choose cross-college and school-wide options alongside the core curriculum, with the programme explicitly noting that electives can come from across RCA and that availability may depend on demand. The elective component supports students in tailoring their pathway, but the page does not present a fixed specialization track; instead, electives broaden practice and can be used to shape individual interests.

positioning.positioning_summary: Information Experience Design MA is positioned as a boundary-breaking, interdisciplinary, research-led programme that combines traditional and emerging media to design experiences of information. It emphasizes speculative and experimental practice, systems thinking, and socially engaged work, while also pointing to professional pathways in startups, cultural and tech sectors, and independent creative practice.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Information Experience Design MA is a 180-credit, one-year full-time programme organized across three terms. The curriculum is built around six named required units: Form & Method, AcrossRCA, Situated Practice, Making Worlds with Others, Core Skills, and an Independent Research Project. The programme emphasizes experimental, transdisciplinary practice, collaborative work, and a final self-directed research project; the page also indicates named pathway areas, but it does not clearly present them as formal specializations.

electives.summary: The programme includes a School-wide elective in Term 2, and the page also describes the degree as using College-wide electives and other cross-school options to support experimentation and collaboration. The evidence does not provide a full elective rule set or a named elective list for this MA, but it does show that electives are part of the curriculum and can broaden study across the RCA.

positioning.positioning_summary: Design Futures is positioned as a research-led, interdisciplinary MDes that uses design futures methods to address complex social, environmental, and strategic uncertainty. The programme emphasizes transdisciplinary practice, research methodology, complex systems, futures thinking, and entrepreneurial/professional skills, with a strong sustainability and ethics orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MDes Design Futures is a 180-credit programme in the School of Design with a mostly fixed three-term structure: Term 1 core units in research and systems, Term 2 work on futures and proposal development, and Term 3 the independent research project. Alongside the core sequence, students choose School-wide and College-wide electives in Terms 1 and 2, making the programme structured but choice-led.

electives.summary: Electives are a major part of the programme and are chosen in Terms 1 and 2 from School-wide and College-wide offerings across RCA schools, campuses, and online. Students rank preferences and are allocated based on availability, with no prerequisite for the Term 2 School-wide electives. The elective structure supports a bespoke pathway and lets students shape the programme around interests such as design resilience, ethics, and innovation, but the page does not present a fixed concentration or formal track.
